title: Carved sycamore cheese board
subtitle: A rectangular serving board with a carved handle and shallow field, from seasoned sycamore
excerpt: A cheese board with a carved integral handle is a simple flat-blank project that produces an elegant kitchen piece. The sycamore blank is shaped to a rounded-rectangle with a protruding handle at one short end; the serving field is recessed 2 to 3 mm below the rim by push cuts; the handle is thinned and rounded. Finished with board butter for immediate kitchen use.
sourceNotes: Handled serving board and cheese board forms documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and in British museum collections of domestic treen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at stable moisture content. Sycamore is the traditional British species for cheese boards and kitchen implements: food-safe, pale, close-grained, and stays flat.
The rough piece of wood from which the finished item is carved. A cheese board blank is a flat section of seasoned sycamore, typically 25 to 30 cm long and 15 to 18 cm wide.
A carving cut in which the blade travels away from the body. Used to remove the shallow field recess and to shape the handle.
A short, controlled carving cut using the off-hand thumb as a pivot. Used for rounding the handle edges and chamfering the board rim.

projectSchedule.title: Shape, carve, and sand the board
projectSchedule.body: Carve the handle profile, recess the field, chamfer edges, sand to 240 grit. One to two hours.

projectSchedule.title: Apply board butter and use immediately
projectSchedule.body: Apply board butter, buff off after twenty minutes. Ready for use the same day.

content.text: A cheese board with a carved handle is a flat-blank project: all the carving work is done with
push cuts
and
thumb-pivot cuts
on the flat
blank
. No hollowing, no deep cuts, no complex structure.
Seasoned
sycamore is the classic material for British kitchen woodwork; it is pale, close-grained, and food-safe. Board butter means the piece is ready for use the same day it is finished.
What you need: wood

A seasoned sycamore blank, 28 cm long, 16 cm wide, 2 cm thick. The blank should be flat; check with a straight edge. One short end of the blank will become the handle; the remaining length becomes the serving field. Beech and lime are equally suitable at the same dimensions.
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife; a leather strop. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grit. Board butter.
The work
Step 1: Mark the layout

Mark the handle zone: the back 8 cm of the blank, 6 to 7 cm wide, centred on the blank width. This leaves a rounded extension at the back end of the blank that will become the handle. Mark the transition between handle and serving field with a curved line: the serving field widens from the handle width to the full blank width over 3 to 4 cm.
Step 2: Shape the handle and board outline

Use push cuts along the sides of the handle section to remove material to the pencilled handle width. The corners at the handle sides should be rounded, not square. Shape the transition curves from handle to serving field with thumb-pivot cuts. Round the three serving field corners (the end opposite the handle and both side front corners) with push cuts to a gentle arc.
Step 3: Thin and round the handle

The handle should be thinner than the serving field so it is comfortable to grip without lifting the board. Thin the handle from both faces with push cuts, reducing it from 2 cm to about 12 to 14 mm. Round the handle edges to an oval section with thumb-pivot cuts along the sides. The grip end of the handle can be rounded to a dome or left slightly flattened.
Step 4: Recess the serving field

Pencil a rim boundary 1 cm in from the edge of the serving field on all three sides (not the handle side). Remove 2 to 3 mm from the inner serving field with push cuts, working from the rim boundary toward the centre. The field must be even in depth; check with a straight edge across it. The rim boundary should be defined with a stop cut around its full length before removing material on the field side.
Step 5: Chamfer all edges

Take thumb-pivot cuts around all outer edges of the serving field and handle to produce a consistent chamfer of 2 to 3 mm. This removes sharp corners and gives the board a finished, considered appearance.
Step 6: Sand
Sand through 120, 180, and 240 grit. Sand the flat serving field along the grain. Sand the handle along its length. Sand the chamfers along the chamfer direction. Wipe away all dust between grits.
Finishing

Apply board butter with a soft cloth, working it into the recessed field and all carved surfaces. Allow to stand for twenty minutes, then buff firmly with a dry cloth. Immediately food-safe. Re-apply monthly.
Variations
Add a chip-carved border pattern on the serving field rim using a chip-carving knife, before board butter is applied.
Make the board circular (25 cm diameter) with a 6 cm handle extension: the same process on a round disc rather than a rectangular blank.
Carve a small hanging hole through the handle end using a hand drill for kitchen wall storage.
Care
Wipe clean with a damp cloth; do not immerse or dishwasher. Dry immediately. Re-apply board butter monthly for a board in regular use.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when shaping and recessing the board.

items.symptom: The handle is not centred; it pulls to one side
items.cause: The pencilled handle centreline was not checked against both sides of the blank before cutting.
items.fix: Measure from both edges to the centreline at the handle neck before starting. Mark both sides of the handle zone and work from both reference lines.

items.symptom: The serving field recess is deeper at the centre than at the rim boundary
items.cause: Push cuts were not kept to a consistent shaving depth across the field.
items.fix: Check field levelness with a straight edge at multiple points. Mark any high spots and take corrective passes.

items.symptom: The handle feels too thin and flexes when gripped
items.cause: More material was removed than the target 12 to 14 mm.
items.fix: A handle that has been thinned too much cannot be corrected. If the flex is slight, the board is still usable; check dimension before thinning the second face in future.
